title: Japanese Shoyu Butter Corn Rice

ingredients.name: short-grain rice or sona masoori rice

subs.name: sona masoori or any medium-grain rice; avoid basmati as it doesn't absorb the corn juices the same way

ingredients.name: sweet corn kernels (fresh, frozen or canned)
visual_checks: fresh corn cut off the cob has the best texture; canned works well drained
ingredients.notes: fresh corn cut off the cob has the best texture; canned works well drained

subs.name: frozen peas or edamame (shelled soya beans) for variety; both work well with the butter-soy base
coconut oil for a dairy-free version with a subtly sweet note, or ghee for a richer Indian-inflected flavour
subs.notes: ghee changes the flavour profile but pairs beautifully with soy

subs.name: coconut aminos plus a pinch of salt for a soy-free version
subs.notes: similar savoury depth with a slightly sweeter note

steps.text: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook with 600 ml water in a covered saucepan over medium-low heat: bring to a boil, stir once, cover tightly, reduce to lowest flame and cook for 12 minutes. Remove from heat and rest, covered, for 10 minutes.

While the rice cooks, melt butter in a wide pan or tawa over medium heat. Add corn kernels in a single layer and cook without stirring for 2 to 3 minutes until the undersides are lightly golden and you can smell a nutty, slightly sweet aroma.

Stir the corn and cook for 1 more minute. Pour the soy sauce directly over the corn and stir quickly — it will spit and caramelise against the hot pan within 30 seconds. Remove the pan from heat immediately.

Fluff the cooked rice with a fork or rice paddle. Add the corn and all the buttery, savoury pan juices to the rice and fold through gently with a scooping motion so the corn is evenly distributed without crushing the grains.

Taste for seasoning — the soy sauce should be sufficient but you can add another splash if needed. Plate and scatter spring onion generously over the top. Finish with a grind of black pepper and serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → coconut oil for a dairy-free version with a subtly sweet note, or ghee for a richer Indian-inflected flavour (ghee changes the flavour profile but pairs beautifully with soy); corn → frozen peas or edamame (shelled soya beans) for variety; both work well with the butter-soy base; soy-sauce → coconut aminos plus a pinch of salt for a soy-free version (similar savoury depth with a slightly sweeter note); rice → sona masoori or any medium-grain rice; avoid basmati as it doesn't absorb the corn juices the same way
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This recipe requires no special equipment — a saucepan for rice and a tawa for the corn is all you need. Amul butter works perfectly. Sweet corn (bhutta dana) is available frozen or canned at any Indian supermarket; you can also cut fresh corn off the cob and use it immediately. Soy sauce is available nationwide.
